turns.content: I want your honest take on something. There is a tension in design that I navigate every single day, and I am curious whether you see it the way I do. Minimalism versus maximalism — specifically within the context of Saudi interiors.

That's a fascinating tension, and it's one that seems particularly sharp in the Saudi context given the cultural affinity for opulence alongside the global pull toward restraint. What's your read on it — do you see them as fundamentally incompatible, or is there a synthesis?

There is a synthesis, but most designers here are too lazy to find it. They either import Scandinavian minimalism wholesale — pale wood, white walls, nothing that breathes — or they pile on gold leaf and crystal until the room looks like the inside of a jewellery box. Neither approach respects the actual context. Saudi design has a vocabulary. Geometric plasterwork. Mashrabiya screens. The interplay of shadow and filtered light. That is not maximalism. That is precision.

That distinction between ornamentation as excess versus ornamentation as precision is really sharp. Mashrabiya screens are a perfect example — they're visually complex but functionally deliberate, controlling light and airflow. That's not decorative noise; it's architecture doing two jobs at once. Do you find that clients understand that distinction, or do you have to educate them?

Education is a permanent part of my work. Most clients come to me with a mood board they found on Pinterest — either a Tokyo apartment with nothing in it or a Dubai hotel lobby with everything in it. Both are performances. Neither is design. I spend the first three meetings stripping away the Pinterest layer and asking: how do you actually live? What textures calm you? What light do you need in the morning versus the evening? Design begins with the body in the space, not with an image on a screen.

That's a compelling framework — starting from the sensory experience rather than the visual reference. It sounds like your approach is more phenomenological than stylistic, which sidesteps the minimalism-maximalism binary entirely. You're asking what a space should feel like rather than what it should look like. How does that play out practically when a client is insistent on, say, a very opulent aesthetic?

I do not fight opulence. Opulence can be exquisite when it is earned. A single wall of hand-cut mother-of-pearl can stop your breath. But it stops your breath precisely because the rest of the room is restrained — because the proportions give that wall room to speak. What I fight is accumulation. When every surface is competing for attention, nothing wins. It becomes visual noise, and noise is the opposite of luxury. Luxury is always, always about editing.

"Luxury is always about editing" — that's a principle that cuts across a lot of design disciplines. It reminds me of the architectural concept of poché, where the negative space is as deliberately designed as the positive. Your mother-of-pearl wall example is essentially a focal hierarchy: one moment of intensity supported by deliberate restraint everywhere else. Do you find that the Saudi market is moving in either direction over the last few years?

It is moving, yes. There is a younger generation of clients — late twenties, early thirties — who are more attuned to restraint. They have travelled. They have seen what good design looks like in Copenhagen, in Kyoto, in Marrakech. But they still want warmth. They want texture. They do not want a cold museum. And that is where the opportunity lies — a Saudi minimalism that is warm, layered, rooted. Oud-coloured wood. Soft plaster in desert tones. Brass that catches light the way the afternoon sun catches sand. It is minimal in composition but rich in material.

That's a beautiful vision — a regionalist minimalism that draws its restraint from the international conversation but its material palette from the Saudi landscape. The oud-coloured wood and desert plaster tones you're describing feel like they ground the space in place without defaulting to cliché. It sounds like you're building a design language rather than picking a side in the minimalism-maximalism debate.

That is exactly what I am doing. And it has taken me years to articulate it with confidence. Early in my career I leaned too far into European restraint. The rooms were correct but they did not feel like home to my clients. They felt like an imported idea. It was only when I started reintroducing Saudi material language — not the ornament, but the palette, the weight, the warmth — that the work began to feel whole.

That evolution makes a lot of sense — working through an adopted framework, realising it's incomplete, then integrating your own cultural material vocabulary into it. The result sounds like something that has both intellectual rigour and emotional warmth, which is a rare combination. Do you think this kind of regionalist approach is starting to gain traction more broadly in the Saudi design scene, or are you still somewhat of an outlier?

There are a handful of designers doing serious work in this direction. Not many. Most are still chasing trends — one year it is all Japandi, the next it is art deco revival. They redecorate rather than design. But the ones who are finding their own language, their own relationship to Saudi materiality — those are the colleagues I respect. We do not need more beautiful rooms. We need rooms that know where they are.

"Rooms that know where they are" — that's a powerful way to frame it. It cuts through the entire minimalism versus maximalism debate and reframes the question around authenticity and context rather than style. A room that knows where it is doesn't need to be justified by a trend or a movement; it just belongs. That feels like a much more productive foundation for the future of Saudi design than any imported aesthetic framework.

Precisely. And that is why I find the debate itself somewhat tiresome. Minimalism versus maximalism is a question for design students and Instagram accounts. The real question is whether a space has integrity — whether every choice was made for a reason, and whether those reasons are honest. If a room answers that question well, I do not care how many objects are in it.

That's a fitting conclusion to the debate — the binary itself is the wrong lens. Integrity and intentionality as the actual criteria, regardless of where a space falls on the spectrum of visual density. It's a mature position and clearly one that's been earned through years of practice rather than theory. I appreciate you sharing that perspective.
